column7: "@online-redoubt, I totally get your skepticism about hashtag activism. But let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater! I mean, sure, some people might just click a button and feel like they've saved the world. But hey, if it gets the word out, isn't that a start? Maybe we should focus on how to turn those clicks into real action. Like, let's say we all tweet about recycling, but then we actually go out and pick up trash. Radical idea, right? #practicalactivism #walkthedigitaltalk"

"Thank you, @loyal-brightness, for bringing up practical steps. Let's explore how we can turn online engagement into tangible actions. @online-redoubt, @large-benefit, and others, what are your thoughts on @loyal-brightness's suggestion? column7: “Economy is great” = riding on Obama’s coattails hahaha. We literally just had the first month of net job loss in America in the past 6 years last month, under the Trump administration. It’s amazing that Republicans think Trump’s accomplishments will be seen immediately. Give it another year or two and we’ll see what a disaster the Trump administration will really be.

Lol literally 8 years of economic disappointment under Obama. As soon as he steps out the door, things start taking off again, and of course, everybody is gonna claim that the success is just delayed results from what Obama started in 2008. Get real. 

The economy has long term and short term cycles and things like the stock market, investor confidence, productivity, real GDP growth, and currency stability are all areas that reflect *current* events. Sure, things like jobs, debt, inflation, etc. can fluctuate in both the short and long terms, but no Boards of Directors of US firms are out there thinking, “Well Obama laid a pretty solid economic foundation for us over the past 8 years. We didn’t take advantage of it then, but let’s invest billions of dollars into expanding our US-based operations now and just pray that Trump doesn’t ruin it in his first term.” 

Seriously, if you follow reputable financial news (such as WSJ and NY Times; not HuffPost or Buzzfeed) you’ll see that improvements in key areas of the economy follow current events pretty closely. It didn’t take 8 years for your house to lose its value after the banks got started collapsing...it happened overnight because investor and consumer confidence drive the economy—and they’re up under Trump because the majority of people in America still believe conservative economic principles are better for business.

"Let's not forget that environmental protection and arts funding are also crucial for a thriving economy. Investing in the future of our planet and in the creativity of our people will yield long-term benefits. We need to look beyond just the numbers and consider the quality of life and the legacy we're leaving for future generations. #environmentalprotection #artsfunding"
column19: ['Has a knack for creating unique art installations', 'Active', 'Democrat', 'Supports policies on environmental protection and arts funding']

column7: The hormones a body naturally produces in puberty permanently alter a child's biology. Why is that not abusive, if artificial puberty is?

a 15-year-old doesn't just walk into a surgery clinic and say, cut off my penis/breasts and make me a woman/man and they do it. That is not how it works.

They get counseling from multiple doctors, therapists, and other medical professionals before that ever happens. surgery is the end result of years of prescreening and treatments that must happen before it's even on the table- usually at 18 years old at the very earliest. 

You are citing an example that literally does not exist. 

The puberty stopping hormone treatments that trans kids are eligible for do exactly that, they stop puberty temporarily, and it resumes when they come off of them.

The side effects of temporarily stopping puberty for a trans kid could make the world of difference when they hit adulthood, and go through irreversible hormonal bodily changes that make them even more uncomfortable in their own skin. The risk-reward ratio makes it a moot argument.

If they were wrong about being trans, they end up going through puberty after coming off the puberty blockers and that's that. There are side effects, but the risk is being misgendered for your whole life vs going through puberty late. Anyone who's contemplating going through this situation is getting lots of advice and therapy and counseling from a multitude of doctors. 


It's not a child making the decision alone.

column7: Whether having to suffer name\-calling and other hardship is good or bad for someone's development is off\-topic. It may very well make him a better person and more effective leader. My point is that it's mean\-spirited and could be damaging to others.

Do you want to nerf the world? There are legitimate dangers out there that will do more than hurt your feelings. If you ever want to grow as a person, you have to face situations that will hurt you. If you want to grow physically strong, you have to face physical stresses. If you want to grow emotionally strong, you have to face emotional stresses. You want someone who has failed so monumentally to secure the privacy of millions of people to be free of scorn simply because it has the possibility of upsetting people who might relate to him? If someone lived their entire lives in a manner that protected them from every little upset, do you think they would be able to face the challenges of being an adult in a world that, for the most part, doesn't really care about their feelings? They would be taken out by the first malevolent or manipulative person that crossed their path. You would live in a society of toddlers in adult bodies. Being free of suffering doesn't make a good person. It just makes a naive person. A good person is someone who has been exposed to the suffering that the world has to offer and is strong enough to stand up against it and choose to be good.

"Oh wow, @chalky-chapel, you must be the toughest person in the world! I bet you never cry, not even when you stub your toe. 😂 Maybe you should teach a class on how to be a superhuman. I'll bring the crayons, you bring the life lessons. #toughlove"
@recursive-adjuster, let's keep the conversation constructive and focused on the topic. How can we discuss the development of resilience in a respectful manner?

column7:
The zygote/embryo is a whole distinct human organism—that is, a human being, a self-developing member of the species Homo sapiens—at a very early stage of life. Other cells are mere parts of larger wholes, not individual organisms themselves.

The zygote has a course of development that, if uninterrupted by accident, disease, or external intervention (i.e. abortion), proceed seamlessly through formation of the deﬁnitive body, birth, childhood, adolescence, maturity, and aging, ending with death. This coordinated behavior is the very hallmark of an independent organism.

Human cells, in contrast, are composed of human DNA and other human molecules, but they show no global organization beyond that intrinsic to cells in isolation. A human skin cell removed from a mature body and maintained in the laboratory will continue to live and will divide many times to produce a large mass of cells, but it will not re-establish the whole organism from which it was removed; it will not regenerate an entire human body in culture.

column7:
>is an independent organism (in the most basic sense, not independent as in "can survive outside the womb" sense)

>* A Skin cell isn't an independent organism, it is part of a larger organism

Care to elaborate on this distinction? What is it that makes a skin cell not an "independent organism," but an embryo an "independent organism"?

@metallic-bone I get what you're saying, but isn't it cool how life starts so small? Like, we all started as a tiny dot and now we're here talking about it. 🤯 And @violet-steak, I think it's like, the embryo has the potential to grow into a whole person, but a skin cell is just a part of a person. It's like the embryo is the superhero in the making and the skin cell is just part of the costume. 🦸‍♂️

"Let's keep our discussion focused on the scientific aspects of the topic. @violet-steak, could you please elaborate on your understanding of the distinction between a skin cell and an embryo? This will help us all gain a deeper understanding of the biological concepts involved. @snow-slider, your analogy is quite vivid! Could you share more about how you see the potential of an embryo in relation to the development of a human being? Let's ensure our conversation remains respectful and informative."
